DecodeLabs Internship 2026 — Project 01

The Empathy Map

Before designing a single pixel, understand the user's real pain — not surface complaints, but the psychological and behavioural reality underneath them.

Deliverable User Persona + Empathy Map
Domain Fitness App — Time-Poor Professionals
Tools Figma / FigJam
Intern Pierre

The Brief

Designing for the Time-Poor Professional

A fitness startup wants to launch an app for busy office professionals who struggle to find time for exercise. The team needs to understand the user's real pain before designing a single screen.

The Problem

Existing fitness apps assume users have 30+ uninterrupted minutes, gym access, and high motivation. The reality for office professionals is the opposite on all three counts.

Workouts too long Interfaces too loud Requires gym access

The Approach

Act as a UX Researcher. Define the target audience, build a specific user persona, and map their full psychological and behavioural reality using a four-quadrant empathy map.

Persona Development Empathy Mapping Behaviour Analysis

User Persona

Meet the User

Generalized data is useless. We need a specific face — the person who will use this app every single day.

Deliverable 01 — Evan Price, 31, Marketing Manager

Empathy Map

Mapping Evan's Reality

The four-quadrant empathy map separates what is externally visible (Says / Does) from what is internally hidden (Thinks / Feels). The gap between these two layers is where the real design problem lives.

Deliverable 02 — Four-Quadrant Empathy Map with External / Internal Split
Key Insight

Evan doesn't lack awareness. He lacks confidence that healthy actions can fit realistically into his day and still make a difference.

This reframes the design problem: the product doesn't need to motivate him — it needs to prove itself worthy of his limited time and rebuild his trust in small actions.

What This Means for Design

From Research to Requirements

Every need in the persona maps directly to a design constraint. This research isn't background context — it's a set of binding decisions for every screen that follows.

01

Fit the gap, not the ideal

No workout can require more than 5 minutes or a change of environment. Every session must be completable from a desk chair with zero equipment.

02

Prove value immediately

No long onboarding. No goal-setting before the first session. The app must show Evan something useful in under 60 seconds or he will delete it — he has already done this twice.

03

Design against guilt, not for motivation

Streaks, missed-day alerts, and progress bars that reset are actively harmful for this user. Every small action must be framed as a win, not a data point in a failure pattern.
